Title: Biochemical and Metabolic Insights into Hyoscyamine Dehydrogenase

Common_Genes.Applied_Promoters: CaMV 35S constitutive promoter for overexpression; 35S promoter driving HDH-RNAi suppression
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Overexpress AbHDH in TA-producing roots/plants to raise hyoscyamine and secondarily increase anisodamine and scopolamine; suitable for in planta metabolic engineering of pharmaceutical tropane alkaloids, potentially stacked with downstream H6H or upstream CYP80F1 depending product goal. Caution: RNAi shows pathway redundancy, so knockout/suppression alone may not reduce product and may only accumulate hyoscyamine aldehyde.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes hyoscyamine biosynthesis; overexpression increases hyoscyamine and also boosts anisodamine and scopolamine, while RNAi causes hyoscyamine aldehyde accumulation without reducing final alkaloids.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transcriptome candidate mining; tissue-expression/qPCR; heterologous expression and purified enzyme assays; LC-HRMS metabolite identification; crystal structure; site-directed mutagenesis; transgenic hairy root overexpression and RNAi with metabolite profiling.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Hairy roots cultured in liquid MS medium for 28 days; in vitro reaction direction influenced by pH and temperature, with physiological plant pH favoring reduction.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Highly expressed in secondary roots, moderately in primary roots, not expressed in stems or leaves.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Highly expressed in secondary roots, moderately in primary roots, low/undetectable in stems and leaves.
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: A. belladonna transcriptome mining/digital gene expression; no accession reported
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: AbHDH is biochemically reversible, catalyzing reciprocal conversion between hyoscyamine aldehyde and hyoscyamine, but strongly favors aldehyde reduction under physiological conditions. RNAi did not lower hyoscyamine, implying functional redundancy by other oxidoreductases in planta and that AbHDH is valuable but replaceable.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: RNAi causes hyoscyamine aldehyde accumulation; overexpression tradeoffs on growth/yield were not reported.
Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Zinc-containing long-chain dehydrogenase/reductase; alcohol dehydrogenase conserved domain PF08240; CAD/SAD-like Rossmann-fold oxidoreductase
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AbHDH was identified as a root-expressed dehydrogenase associated with tropane alkaloid biosynthesis in Atropa belladonna. 2. Purified AbHDH reduces hyoscyamine aldehyde to hyoscyamine and can reversibly oxidize hyoscyamine back to the aldehyde, but reduction is strongly favored under physiological conditions. 3. Overexpression in hairy roots significantly increases hyoscyamine and consequently anisodamine and scopolamine, whereas RNAi only elevates hyoscyamine aldehyde, indicating engineering value but in planta redundancy.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Roots, especially cultured hairy roots/secondary roots
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Pharmaceutical anticholinergic drug; precursor supporting production of anisodamine and scopolamine.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: CaMV 35S constitutive promoter for overexpression; 35S promoter driving HDH-RNAi suppression
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: A strong candidate engineering target for in planta enhancement of pharmaceutical tropane alkaloids. Overexpression raises hyoscyamine supply and thereby improves downstream anisodamine/scopolamine accumulation. Best deployed in root cultures or TA-producing tissues, potentially combined with H6H for scopolamine-focused production.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces hyoscyamine aldehyde to hyoscyamine using NADPH; under alternative conditions also oxidizes hyoscyamine to hyoscyamine aldehyde with NADP+.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Increases hyoscyamine production and indirectly increases anisodamine and scopolamine when overexpressed; suppression leads to hyoscyamine aldehyde accumulation but not reduction in downstream alkaloids.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Purified recombinant enzyme assays with CYP80F1-generated substrate; LC-HRMS product detection; kinetic analysis; deuterium-labeling to show pro-R hydride transfer from NADPH; crystal structure and docking; catalytic-residue mutagenesis; transgenic hairy root metabolite profiling.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Reduction optimum pH 6.4 and 40°C; oxidation optimum pH 10.4 and 45°C; physiological plant pH around 7.2 favors reduction toward hyoscyamine.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Highly expressed in secondary roots, moderate in primary roots, absent/very low in stems and leaves.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Highly expressed in secondary roots, moderate in primary roots, absent/very low in stems and leaves.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Transcriptome-based candidate mining and digital gene expression; no accession reported
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Crystal structure deposited as PDB 7CGU. AbHDH is a zinc-dependent long-chain dehydrogenase with catalytic Zn2+ coordinated by Cys52, His74, Glu75, and Cys168. Ser54 is critical for proton shuttle; Cys100 helps stabilize substrate. Despite direct catalytic role, the step is not genetically essential in planta due to apparent redundant enzymes.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Late hyoscyamine-forming module of tropane alkaloid pathway
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Suppression accumulates hyoscyamine aldehyde; no growth penalty or yield penalty reported for overexpression lines.
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Zinc-dependent long-chain alcohol dehydrogenase/reductase, CAD/SAD-like, Rossmann-fold NADPH-binding enzyme, PF08240
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as a useful late-pathway control point for engineering, but not established as sole rate-limiting or essential step because RNAi did not reduce end products.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AbHDH catalyzes the final reduction of hyoscyamine aldehyde to hyoscyamine in the tropane alkaloid pathway. 2. The enzyme is reversible in vitro but reduction is thermodynamically and kinetically favored under physiological conditions. 3. Overexpression in A. belladonna hairy roots significantly increases hyoscyamine and downstream anisodamine/scopolamine, confirming pathway engineering value.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Roots, especially cultured hairy roots/secondary roots
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Anticholinergic pharmaceutical alkaloid and precursor for anisodamine/scopolamine.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Yeast expression vector pYES2 for functional assay in WAT11; native promoter not studied
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful upstream engineering target to generate hyoscyamine aldehyde precursor pool, but prior overexpression alone did not alter total hyoscyamine production, implying downstream steps such as HDH can be more limiting. Potentially combine with HDH and H6H in multi-gene stacks.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes littorine carbon rearrangement/oxidation to form hyoscyamine aldehyde.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supplies the direct precursor hyoscyamine aldehyde for hyoscyamine formation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Ectopic expression in yeast WAT11 microsomes with littorine feeding and LC-HRMS detection of hyoscyamine aldehyde; cited prior metabolic engineering evidence.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Microsomal assay in yeast; littorine feeding required for detection.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Known TA biosynthesis gene highly/specially expressed in roots according to transcriptome comparison, but precise details not given here.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Root-associated expression in A. belladonna implied from known TA biosynthesis genes.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Referenced transcriptome expression comparison; no accession reported
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Used experimentally to generate unstable hyoscyamine aldehyde in situ for AbHDH assays. Prior overexpression reportedly did not alter overall hyoscyamine production, implying non-sufficiency alone.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Overexpression alone may not raise hyoscyamine because downstream reduction can remain limiting.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Not established; evidence suggests not sufficient alone to increase end-product accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYP80F1 converts littorine to hyoscyamine aldehyde, the direct precursor of hyoscyamine. 2. In this study, AbCYP80F1-expressing yeast microsomes were used to generate hyoscyamine aldehyde for demonstrating AbHDH reduction activity. 3. Previous engineering results cited in the paper indicate CYP80F1 overexpression alone does not increase overall hyoscyamine accumulation.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Not specified in this study; discussed from previous reports including overexpression in plants/root cultures
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong downstream engineering target for scopolamine-oriented production; should be stacked with HDH when increasing total flux from hyoscyamine toward anisodamine/scopolamine is desired.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates hyoscyamine to anisodamine and further epoxidizes/oxidatively converts anisodamine to scopolamine.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Uses increased hyoscyamine supply to promote anisodamine and scopolamine accumulation; previous overexpression can markedly raise scopolamine and convert much hyoscyamine to scopolamine.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Evidence cited from prior overexpression studies; indirect support in current study from increased anisodamine/scopolamine following HDH overexpression.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms downstream target products from hyoscyamine
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Not specifically reported in this paper
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Known TA biosynthesis gene highly expressed in roots according to context; no direct data here.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Paper notes H6H from Hyoscyamus niger markedly increases scopolamine production in several TA-producing plants and can convert hyoscyamine almost completely to scopolamine in leaves.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May deplete hyoscyamine when scopolamine is the favored product; no additional tradeoffs discussed here.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as a major downstream control point for scopolamine production in prior work.
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: TA-producing plants including Hyoscyamus niger and Atropa belladonna
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. H6H is the downstream enzyme converting hyoscyamine toward anisodamine and scopolamine. 2. The paper infers that increased hyoscyamine generated by HDH overexpression provides more substrate for H6H, elevating both anisodamine and scopolamine. 3. Prior studies cited establish H6H as an effective engineering target for scopolamine-rich production.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Roots and leaves in engineered plants
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Pharmaceutical tropane alkaloid with anticholinergic activity.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Not specified in this study; discussed from prior reports
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for increasing upstream precursor N-methylputrescine, but inadequate alone to increase hyoscyamine/scopolamine; should be combined with downstream pathway genes if used in engineering.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes methylation of putrescine to form N-methylputrescine, an early precursor in tropane alkaloid biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Raises N-methylputrescine but not necessarily hyoscyamine or scopolamine when overexpressed alone.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Prior metabolic engineering studies cited by the paper.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Known TA biosynthesis gene highly/specially expressed in roots according to transcriptome context.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Cited as an example showing that engineering early steps may not increase final TA products because control can lie downstream.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Early polyamine-derived entry step to tropane alkaloids
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Flux increase may be trapped upstream without downstream engineering.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Not a sufficient control point for hyoscyamine/scopolamine accumulation according to cited overexpression studies.
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Atropa belladonna and related TA-producing plants
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PMT acts in an upstream early step of tropane alkaloid biosynthesis to generate N-methylputrescine. 2. Prior overexpression increased N-methylputrescine but not hyoscyamine or scopolamine. 3. This contrast supports the conclusion that downstream steps such as HDH can be more informative engineering targets for increasing pharmaceutical TAs.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Not specified; discussed as prior suppression evidence
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Demonstrates that some upstream steps are essential and that isozyme redundancy must be considered in engineering. Not a direct target in this study but useful as caution for pathway editing strategies.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes transamination of phenylalanine to phenylpyruvic acid in the upstream route feeding tropane alkaloid biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Suppression considerably reduces downstream phenyllactic acid, hyoscyamine, and scopolamine, despite unchanged phenylalanine and phenylpyruvic acid due to isozyme redundancy/cell-specific expression.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Prior gene suppression studies cited in discussion.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Not directly reported here; discussion suggests cell-specific expression.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Mentioned as an example of pathway-gene suppression phenotype and redundancy in TA biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Isozyme redundancy may obscure engineering outcomes.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Important upstream step but subject to redundancy; exact control status not established here.
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Likely Atropa/Root-producing TA plant context from cited work
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Not clearly reported in current text
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ArAT4 is an upstream precursor-supply enzyme in tropane alkaloid biosynthesis. 2. Its suppression reduces downstream alkaloid accumulation, illustrating how key pathway genes can affect hyoscyamine/scopolamine output. 3. The cited redundancy among isozymes provides a conceptual parallel for why HDH suppression may fail to lower hyoscyamine in planta.
